evidence_quotes.quote: digital storefronts are able to deprive their customers and consumers their paid-for items quite easily when they decide they don't want things available anymore for any reason.
Or publishers of books and newspapers going around into peoples homes to take back the books and newspapers which their readers subscribed to or purchased.

paragraphs.text: Digital items, tools, entertainment, and so on should be treated with similar respect as physical items being purchased and subscribed to. As it stands, digital purchases can be taken away from customers and consumers with little recourse when support ends without much recourse. This is especially true with planned obsolescence items, which is also a problem with physical electronics these days. What matters here is that customers and consumers who buy a tool, game, a movie, an audio book, an office suite, a scientific tool, etc. should have the opportunity to continue supporting their own purchases akin to the right to repair your own vehicles, electronics, machinery, etc. when official support from the vendors and producers are terminated. It's also when these things are at "End of Life". With things that get rolling releases this is less of a problem because often customers get the offer for an update to that either free of charge or for a fee, and the old version usually still work. This is not necessarily true for merchandise which is either specialized or just simply have little commercial and monetary value, yet still have great value for those who use it. Scientists needing to rely on decades old computers whose components are no longer in production because they are unable to port their digital tools to modern systems and there being no viable alternatives is a real problem. This is also a problem in the digital entertainment industry, which involved video games, music, shows, and films. People should rightfully expect that their purchases should be available to them at the point of currency changing hands. As it stands, digital storefronts are able to deprive their customers and consumers their paid-for items quite easily when they decide they don't want things available anymore for any reason. As an example, it would be outrageous for car companies to go and take people's cars away from them just because they decided to cease production of the models. Or publishers of books and newspapers going around into peoples homes to take back the books and newspapers which their readers subscribed to or purchased. However, these publishers and vendors are fully capable of doing this to digital goods with impunity if they so wish, or worse, if they think they could sell it again at a mark-up. It's poor faith business practices and ought to be restricted and opposed for the sake of us regular people who just want to have a good time, enjoy media, work on our passion projects, and our academic pursuits.
